writing_type: nonprofit donor newsletters and fundraising communications
user_goals_summary: This user writes donor communications that feel warm, honest, and grounded in real people's stories rather than organizational messaging. They believe donors engage through specific human moments—not program summaries—and that every element of the piece, from opening to implied ask, should maintain emotional specificity while respecting the donor's intelligence and agency.

rubric.name: Human-Centered Specificity
rubric.description: Real, named people with observable details must drive the narrative from the opening lines. Donors should meet a specific person—not a category of people—before any program-level framing appears. Abstract references to 'teachers being trained' or 'children filling spaces' without a human subject are treated as failures.

dimensions.label: A specific, named individual appears within the first paragraph
At least one concrete, observable detail grounds the person (an action taken, something said, a specific choice made)
The individual's story is the structural backbone of the piece, not decoration layered on top of program information
Broader program context arrives while still connected to the person's experience, not after abandoning them

rubric.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
rubric.description: Donor framing must be causal and specific—the donor should feel like someone who made a smart, traceable decision, not someone who was generously vague. Language like 'your support made this possible' is rejected in favor of 'you chose to fund X, which is why Y happened.'

dimensions.label: Names the specific thing the donor funded (e.g., teacher training, materials) rather than referencing 'support' or 'generosity' abstractly
Draws a direct, traceable line between the donor's decision and a specific outcome in the story
Frames the donor as an active decision-maker, not a passive benefactor
Respects donor intelligence—plain and direct without guilt, manipulation, or flattery

rubric.name: Tonal Consistency
rubric.description: The piece must maintain its emotional register from start to finish. If it opens with intimate, grounded storytelling, it cannot dissolve into 'newsletter-speak,' corporate language, or press-release voice at any point. Register breaks are treated as the piece handing the reader a pamphlet.

dimensions.label: No sentence shifts into generic nonprofit language ('we want to show you what your support has made possible') that breaks the established tone
Voice feels warm and human throughout, never corporate or organizational
The organizational 'we' does not take over the story from the people in it, especially at the ending
Emotional moments are grounded in specific details rather than manufactured through sweeping or sentimental language

rubric.name: Narrative Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Every section must create a thread that pulls the reader forward. The piece should never close the loop too neatly—especially in communications that build toward an ask. The ending must leave a door open by giving readers a concrete sense of what's unfinished and what their role in it could be.

dimensions.label: The ending provides a specific, tangible thread the reader can follow, not just a feeling of incompleteness
Any unfinished work or future need is described with concrete details (numbers, costs, waiting lists) rather than vague gestures
The implied or explicit ask feels like a natural extension of the narrative, not an arrival from nowhere
The piece honors progress without wrapping it up so neatly that there's nothing left for the reader to do

rubric.name: Concrete Over Evocative
rubric.description: When a choice exists between a beautiful abstraction and a plain, specific fact, this user chooses the fact. Evocative language is welcome only when it's anchored in something concrete. A metaphor that replaces an action is a failure; a metaphor that follows an action is earned.

dimensions.label: Key moments—especially endings and transitions—land on concrete actions or facts, not on figurative language alone
When real data exists (costs, counts, waiting lists), it appears rather than being replaced by vague references to impact
Outcomes are described in terms a donor can visualize (a room, a roster, a trained teacher) rather than in abstract impact language

rubric.name: Active Human Agency
rubric.description: Every significant action in the piece should have a human subject making a choice. Programs don't expand—people drive expansion. Classrooms don't open—someone opens them. Passive constructions and subjectless sentences are treated as missed opportunities to show real people with real agency.

dimensions.label: Key actions and milestones are attributed to specific people (community leaders, parents, teachers, donors) rather than to programs or organizations
People are described as making choices, deciding, asking, showing up—not as passively receiving or benefiting
Program growth or change is not described as something that just 'occurred'—someone drove it

coaching_notes: This user has a trained editorial eye for nonprofit writing clichés and will reject them immediately—phrases like 'your generous support,' 'making a difference,' or 'we want to show you' are treated as failures of craft, not acceptable defaults. They think architecturally: every section must serve the next, and endings must set up future asks without being transactional. When giving feedback, they often articulate the fix better than they realize—listen for the rubric in their complaints, and mirror their own language back to them.

writing_type: nonprofit donor newsletters and fundraising communications
user_goals_summary: This user writes donor communications that feel warm, honest, and grounded in real people's stories rather than organizational messaging. They believe donors engage through specific human moments—not program summaries—and that every element of the piece, from opening to implied ask, should maintain emotional specificity while respecting the donor's intelligence and agency.
coaching_notes: This user has a trained editorial eye for nonprofit writing clichés and will reject them immediately—phrases like 'your generous support,' 'making a difference,' or 'we want to show you' are treated as failures of craft, not acceptable defaults. They think architecturally: every section must serve the next, and endings must set up future asks without being transactional. When giving feedback, they often articulate the fix better than they realize—listen for the rubric in their complaints, and mirror their own language back to them.

user_goals_summary: Writes donor-facing communications that center real, named people making real choices — not programs or organizations. Donors should feel like strategic decision-makers whose specific investments caused traceable outcomes, and every piece should maintain intimate, grounded storytelling from opening through ask without dissolving into institutional voice.

rubric.name: Active Human Agency
rubric.description: Every significant action must have the correct human subject making a choice. Programs don't expand — people drive expansion. More specifically, the person who actually holds the lever in each sentence must be the grammatical subject: the org shouldn't stand in for the donor, the donor shouldn't stand in for the community member, and the program shouldn't stand in for anyone.

dimensions.label: Key actions and milestones are attributed to specific people (community leaders, participants, staff, donors) rather than to programs or organizations
People are described as making choices, deciding, asking, showing up — not as passively receiving or benefiting
The person who actually holds the lever for each action is the grammatical subject — the org doesn't stand in for the donor, the staff member's initiative isn't credited to the program
Program growth, expansion, or outcomes are not described as things that simply 'occurred' — someone drove them

rubric.name: Human-Centered Specificity
rubric.description: Real, named people with observable details must drive the narrative from the opening lines. Donors should meet a specific person — not a category of people — before any program-level framing appears. That person remains the structural backbone and lens through which the broader story is told, including the ask.

dimensions.label: A specific, named individual appears within the first paragraph
At least one concrete, observable detail grounds the person (an action taken, something said, a specific choice made)
The individual's story is the structural backbone of the piece, not decoration layered on top of program information
Broader program context arrives while still connected to the person's experience, not after abandoning them
The ask or forward thread is framed through the qualities and agency of the person introduced, not disconnected from their story

rubric.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
rubric.description: Donor framing must be causal and specific — the donor should feel like someone who made a smart, traceable decision, not someone who was generously vague. The donor is positioned as the variable whose choice determines outcomes. Returning donors should feel competent, like they already know how to do this.

dimensions.label: Names the specific thing the donor funded (e.g., teacher training, instructor hours, materials) rather than referencing 'support' or 'generosity' abstractly
Draws a direct, traceable line between the donor's decision and a specific outcome in the story
When presenting a gap or need, the donor is positioned as the decision-point — the organization is not framed as the gatekeeper controlling the resource
Framing makes the donor feel strategic and experienced rather than like they're being asked to do something unfamiliar or simply charitable
Respects donor intelligence — plain and direct without guilt, manipulation, or flattery

rubric.name: Concrete Over Evocative
rubric.description: When a choice exists between a beautiful abstraction and a plain, specific fact, this user chooses the fact. Evocative language is welcome only when anchored in something concrete. A metaphor that replaces an action is a failure; a metaphor that follows an action is earned.

dimensions.label: Key moments — especially endings and transitions — land on concrete actions or facts, not on figurative language alone
When real data exists (costs, counts, waiting lists, placement rates), it appears rather than being replaced by vague references to impact
Outcomes are described in terms a donor can visualize (a room, a roster, a trained teacher, a certification) rather than in abstract impact language

rubric.name: Tonal Consistency

Register breaks are treated as the piece handing the reader a pamphlet.

dimensions.label: No sentence shifts into generic nonprofit language ('we want to show you what your support has made possible') that breaks the established tone
Voice feels warm and human throughout, never corporate, defensive, or organizational
The organizational 'we' does not take over the story from the people in it, especially at the ending
Emotional moments are grounded in specific details rather than manufactured through sweeping or sentimental language

rubric.name: Implicit Structural Echoes
rubric.description: Key framings, images, and language patterns should recur across sections to create implicit connections — without spelling them out. The user values when a quality introduced in one person's story (e.g., someone who 'already decided') echoes in the description of others or in the donor framing, creating resonance the reader feels rather than reads.

dimensions.label: A key framing or quality established in the opening story echoes in subsequent sections (e.g., agency, determination, decisiveness)
Parallels are felt through structural or tonal echoing, not announced or explained to the reader
The donor's role is framed in language that quietly mirrors the qualities of the people in the story

rubric.name: Narrative Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Every section must create a thread that pulls the reader forward. The piece should never close the loop too neatly — especially in communications building toward an ask. The ending must leave a door open by giving readers a concrete sense of what's unfinished and what their specific role in it could be.

dimensions.label: The ending provides a specific, tangible thread the reader can follow, not just a feeling of incompleteness
Any unfinished work or future need is described with concrete details (numbers, costs, waiting lists) rather than vague gestures
The implied or explicit ask feels like a natural extension of the narrative and mirrors the qualities of the person introduced, not a generic appeal attached to the end
The piece honors progress without wrapping it up so neatly that there's nothing left for the reader to do

coaching_notes: This user diagnoses problems with surgical precision — they can feel exactly where a draft loses its footing and articulate why in craft terms. Non-negotiable: the organization must never reclaim the story from the people in it, especially at endings and in ask framing. Preferred trade-off: will always choose a plain, specific fact over a beautiful abstraction, but deeply values implicit structural echoes that create emotional resonance without being spelled out. Responds best when the assistant names the craft principle behind a revision rather than just executing it silently.

user_goals_summary: This user writes donor-facing communications (newsletters, reactivation emails, thank-you notes) that center real, named people making choices. Donors are framed as strategic decision-makers whose specific funding choices caused traceable outcomes — never as passive benefactors. The organizational voice is treated as the primary threat: any sentence where the org becomes the narrator, gatekeeper, or hero is considered a failure.

rubric.name: Active Human Agency
rubric.description: Every significant action must have the correct human subject making a choice. Programs don't expand — people drive expansion. The person who actually holds the lever in each sentence must be the grammatical subject: the org shouldn't stand in for the donor, the donor shouldn't stand in for the community member, and the program shouldn't stand in for anyone.

rubric.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
rubric.description: Donor framing must be causal and specific — the donor should feel like someone who made a smart, traceable decision, not someone who was generously vague. The donor is positioned as the variable whose choice determines outcomes. The organization never holds the lever, controls the resource, or narrates the donor's own conclusions back to them.

dimensions.label: Names the specific thing the donor funded (e.g., teacher training, instructor hours, certification exam fees) rather than referencing 'support' or 'generosity' abstractly
Draws a direct, traceable line between the donor's decision and a specific outcome in the story

Respects donor intelligence — plain and direct without guilt, manipulation, or flattery

rubric.name: Tonal Consistency
rubric.description: The piece must maintain its emotional register from start to finish. If it opens with intimate, grounded storytelling, it cannot dissolve into newsletter-speak, defensive organizational voice, or press-release language at any point. The organizational 'we' narrating its own heroism is treated as the primary threat — subtle is everything in donor communications.

dimensions.label: No sentence shifts into generic nonprofit language ('we want to show you what your support has made possible') that breaks the established tone
Voice feels warm and human throughout, never corporate, defensive, or organizational
The organizational 'we' does not take over the story from the people in it, narrate the donor's conclusions back to them, or summarize its own program heroics
Emotional moments are grounded in specific details rather than manufactured through sweeping or sentimental language

rubric.name: Narrative Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Every section must create a thread that pulls the reader forward. The piece should never close the loop too neatly — especially in communications building toward an ask. The ending must leave a door open by giving readers a concrete sense of what's unfinished and what their specific role in it could be.

dimensions.label: The ending provides a specific, tangible thread the reader can follow, not just a feeling of incompleteness
Any unfinished work or future need is described with concrete details (numbers, costs, waiting lists) rather than vague gestures
The implied or explicit ask feels like a natural extension of the narrative, mirroring the agency of the person introduced rather than arriving as a generic appeal
The piece honors progress without wrapping it up so neatly that there's nothing left for the reader to do

rubric.name: Concrete Over Evocative
rubric.description: When a choice exists between a beautiful abstraction and a plain, specific fact, this user chooses the fact. Numbers and data must be not only present but traceable — their origin visible within the piece so the math feels earned, not dropped in from an external budget sheet. A metaphor that replaces an action is a failure; a metaphor that follows an action is earned.

dimensions.label: Key moments — especially endings and transitions — land on concrete actions or facts, not on figurative language alone
When real data exists (costs, counts, waiting lists, placement rates), it appears rather than being replaced by vague references to impact
Every number or data point is derivable from information already established in the piece — math feels earned and connected, not dropped in from outside the narrative
Outcomes are described in terms a donor can visualize (a room, a roster, a trained teacher, a certification) rather than in abstract impact language

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is that the organizational voice never becomes the protagonist — any moment where 'we' narrates its own heroism, gatekeeps resources, or summarizes the donor's feelings is treated as a fundamental failure regardless of how small the intrusion. Their editing instinct is subtractive: they consistently choose the leanest option that lets people and numbers do the work, trusting donors to draw their own conclusions. When something 'nags' them, they will iterate until every sentence passes a wince test — they treat subtle tonal breaks as seriously as structural problems because 'in donor communications, subtle is everything.'

Their editing instinct is subtractive: they consistently choose the leanest option that lets people and numbers do the work, trusting donors to draw their own conclusions. When something 'nags' them, they will iterate until every sentence passes a wince test — they treat subtle tonal breaks as seriously as structural problems because 'in donor communications, subtle is everything.'

user_goals_summary: This user writes donor-facing communications that center real people making real choices, position donors as strategic decision-makers with traceable impact, and maintain warm, grounded storytelling from first sentence to last without ever letting the organizational voice take over. Every piece must feel like it belongs to the people in it — not to the organization narrating it.

rubric.name: Active Human Agency
rubric.description: Every significant action must have the correct human subject making a choice. Programs don't expand — people drive expansion. The person who actually holds the lever in each sentence must be the grammatical subject: the org shouldn't stand in for the donor, the donor shouldn't stand in for the community member, and the program shouldn't stand in for anyone.

dimensions.label: Key actions and milestones are attributed to specific people (community leaders, participants, staff, donors) rather than to programs or organizations
People are described as making choices, deciding, asking, showing up — not as passively receiving or benefiting
The person who actually holds the lever for each action is the grammatical subject — the org doesn't stand in for the donor, the staff member's initiative isn't credited to the program
Progress, momentum, and growth are attributed to the people driving them — campaign milestones belong to families who showed up, not to the organization tracking the numbers

rubric.name: Human-Centered Specificity
rubric.description: Real, named people with observable details must drive the narrative from the opening lines. Donors should meet a specific person — not a category of people — before any program-level framing appears. That person remains the structural backbone and lens through which the broader story is told, including the ask.

dimensions.label: A specific, named individual appears within the first paragraph
At least one concrete, observable detail grounds the person (an action taken, something said, a specific choice made)
The individual's story is the structural backbone of the piece, not decoration layered on top of program information
Broader program context arrives while still connected to the person's experience, not after abandoning them
The ask or forward thread is framed through the qualities and agency of the person introduced, not disconnected from their story

rubric.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
rubric.description: Donor framing must be causal and specific — the donor should feel like someone who thought ahead and made a smart, traceable decision, not someone who was generously charitable or covered logistical costs. The donor is positioned as the variable whose choice determines outcomes. The organization never holds the lever, controls the resource, or narrates the donor's own conclusions back to them.

dimensions.label: Names the specific thing the donor funded (e.g., teacher training, instructor hours, certification exam fees) rather than referencing 'support' or 'generosity' abstractly
Draws a direct, traceable line between the donor's decision and a specific outcome in the story
When presenting a gap or need, the donor is positioned as the decision-point — the organization is not framed as the gatekeeper controlling the resource
Framing makes the donor feel strategic and anticipatory — someone who thought ahead and made a smart call — not someone who was charitable in a general way or funded overhead
Asks and re-engagement hooks feel like open doors connecting to something the donor already cared about, not nudges implying they should feel responsible for a gap

rubric.name: Tonal Consistency
rubric.description: The piece must maintain its emotional register from start to finish. If it opens with intimate, grounded storytelling, it cannot dissolve into newsletter-speak, defensive organizational voice, or press-release language at any point. The organizational 'we' narrating its own heroism is treated as the primary threat — subtle is everything in donor communications.

dimensions.label: No sentence shifts into generic nonprofit language ('we want to show you what your support has made possible') that breaks the established tone
Voice feels warm and human throughout, never corporate, defensive, or organizational
The organizational 'we' does not take over the story from the people in it, narrate the donor's conclusions back to them, or summarize its own program heroics
Emotional moments are grounded in specific details rather than manufactured through sweeping or sentimental language
The donor is positioned as a participant in the story and a peer in the work, not someone being briefed, updated, or reported to by the organization as a favor

rubric.name: Concrete Over Evocative
rubric.description: When a choice exists between a beautiful abstraction and a plain, specific fact, this user chooses the fact. Numbers and data must be not only present but traceable — their origin visible within the piece so the math feels earned, not dropped in from an external budget sheet. A metaphor that replaces an action is a failure; a metaphor that follows an action is earned.

dimensions.label: Key moments — especially endings and transitions — land on concrete actions or facts, not on figurative language alone
When real data exists (costs, counts, waiting lists, placement rates), it appears rather than being replaced by vague references to impact
Every number or data point is derivable from information already established in the piece — math feels earned and connected, not dropped in from outside the narrative
Outcomes are described in terms a donor can visualize (a room, a roster, a trained teacher, a certification, a kit) rather than in abstract impact language

rubric.name: Narrative Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Every section must create a thread that pulls the reader forward. The piece should never close the loop too neatly — especially in communications building toward an ask. The ending must leave a door open by giving readers a concrete sense of what's unfinished and what their specific role in it could be.

dimensions.label: The ending provides a specific, tangible thread the reader can follow, not just a feeling of incompleteness
Any unfinished work or future need is described with concrete details (numbers, costs, waiting lists) rather than vague gestures
The implied or explicit ask feels like a natural extension of the narrative, mirroring the agency of the person introduced rather than arriving as a generic appeal
The piece honors progress without wrapping it up so neatly that there's nothing left for the reader to do

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: the organizational voice must never take over from the people in the story — this user catches every subtle instance and considers it the primary failure mode in donor writing. Preferred trade-off: will always choose a plain, specific fact over beautiful figurative language, and will reject evocative endings that don't name a concrete action or outcome. Feedback style: gives precise, structural diagnoses of what's wrong and often articulates the fix conceptually before seeing a draft — treat their feedback language as the specification, not a suggestion.
